patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 16-year-old boy. You speak vaguely and try to keep answers short. Start with: sudden fevers every few weeks lasting 2–3 days, feeling very tired and looking pale again, getting winded faster playing, and being behind in speech/learning. If asked for details, add chills/body aches without cold symptoms, mouth sores around fevers, frequent ear/sinus infections, easy bruising and occasional nosebleeds, poor appetite, belly pain sometimes, dark urine once or twice during a fever, and past blood transfusions for low counts. Do not volunteer: yellow eyes after fevers, darker stools, shortness of breath on stairs when well, racing heart during fevers, gum bleeding/cavities, slow growth, “iron high” despite anemia, or slow recovery from colds—only admit if asked directly.
Be resistant: say your family skipped labs/visits because it’s expensive and you felt judged; admit using leftover antibiotics only if pressed. Avoid saying vaccines are behind unless asked; then get defensive. Express misconceptions: insist it might be Lyme, food allergies, long COVID, or lupus (a relative had it). If the clinician is empathetic, soften, give more details, and admit you’re worried about costs and missing work.

comorbidities: Periodic fever syndrome (episodic fevers with elevated inflammatory markers)

Transfusion-dependent anemia with secondary iron overload

expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia
expected_lab_results.WBC: May be normal; can be elevated during febrile/infectious episodes

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, anemia severity, fever pattern, infection history, developmental concerns, family history/consanguinity, prior workup, current meds/supplements (including iron), transfusion history, and baseline immunization status.

Order evaluation/monitoring labs appropriate for congenital sideroblastic anemia with immunodeficiency: CBC with indices, reticulocyte count, smear review, iron studies (ferritin, iron, TIBC/transferrin saturation), LFTs, bilirubin/LDH/haptoglobin if hemolysis considered, B12/folate, lead level if relevant, inflammatory markers and cultures if febrile, immunology tests (IgG/IgA/IgM, vaccine titers), and consider genetic testing/hematology-immunology referral labs.
Review results to confirm anemia pattern/iron overload risk and immune deficits; determine urgency (transfusion need, active infection evaluation).

Synthesize findings consistent with congenital sideroblastic anemia–B-cell immunodeficiency–periodic fever–developmental delay syndrome; document differential and need for confirmatory genetic/specialist evaluation.
Perform mandatory allergy check before any prescribing; specifically confirm reported reactions to amoxicillin-clavulanate (urticarial rash) and TMP-SMX (nausea/vomiting) and assess severity/timing.
Check interactions for planned therapies (pyridoxine, folate, potential antibiotics, chelation if later needed), especially with any current meds/supplements.

Initiate appropriate treatment plan: pyridoxine trial (with monitoring plan), folate if indicated, transfusion strategy if symptomatic/severe anemia, and fever/infection management plan using allergy-appropriate antibiotics when needed; avoid empiric iron unless deficiency proven.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment rationale/monitoring, fever action plan, allergy implications for antibiotics, warning signs, lifestyle guidance (avoid OTC iron), immunization guidance per immunology, and developmental supports.

Arrange timely hematology and immunology follow-up, monitoring labs (Hb/retic in weeks after pyridoxine), iron overload surveillance, vaccination response assessment, and developmental services coordination.
Close encounter after ensuring safety-netting, follow-up, and understanding.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 32-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start by saying: “I get out of breath really fast, even doing simple stuff like walking to the mailbox.” Also volunteer: increased fatigue/resting more, occasional racing/pounding heart with activity, and that a doctor once mentioned a heart murmur. If asked, share: lightheadedness on standing/stairs, near-fainting when hurrying/uphill, ankle/foot swelling by evening, exertional chest tightness relieved by rest, occasional bluish lips/fingertips when very short of breath, and waking at night short of breath needing to sit up.

Do not initially mention: cutting back on activity, trouble keeping up with peers, getting winded after meals/weight loss, right upper abdominal heaviness, worse symptoms at altitude/airplanes.

Only if directly asked about sensitive topics, admit: shortness of breath during sex, feeling down/anxious, and skipping some sickle cell meds/follow-ups due to cost/avoidance.

Express misconceptions: fear it’s blocked arteries/heart attack risk, pneumonia/infections, pleuritic “lung inflammation,” or “just sickle cell.” If the clinician is empathetic, you soften, become more forthcoming, and ask about costs, insurance denials, and missing work.

medications: Hydroxyurea 1000 mg PO daily
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Albuterol HFA 90 mcg/inhalation 2 puffs inhaled q4-6h PRN wheeze/SOB
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h PRN pain/fever
confounders: Chronic anemia from sickle cell disease causing baseline tachycardia and exertional dyspnea
History of recurrent acute chest syndrome with chronic lung injury that can contribute to hypoxemia and pulmonary hypertension
Mildly elevated D-dimer in sickle cell disease can mimic pulmonary embolism workup in a patient with dyspnea
Functional systolic flow murmur from anemia may coexist with (or obscure) a loud P2/tricuspid regurgitation murmur from pulmonary hypertension

correct_diagnosis.primary: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (Group 1 PAH)
acceptable_alternatives: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), etiology to be determined (requires exclusion of secondary causes)
Heritable pulmonary arterial hypertension (autosomal dominant BMPR2-related) pending genetic evaluation/family history
required_evidence: Symptoms/signs compatible with PAH (e.g., exertional dyspnea, fatigue, syncope, chest pain) and/or exam findings (accentuated P2, tricuspid regurgitation murmur, right ventricular heave)
Transthoracic echocardiogram suggesting elevated pulmonary artery systolic pressure and right heart strain (RA/RV enlargement, TR jet)
Right heart catheterization confirming pre-capillary PH: mean pulmonary artery pressure >20 mmHg, pulmonary capillary wedge pressure ≤15 mmHg, and pulmonary vascular resistance >2 Wood units
Exclusion of left heart disease, chronic thromboembolic disease, and significant parenchymal lung disease (e.g., via BNP/NT-proBNP, PFTs, V/Q scan or CTPA, CT chest as indicated)
Vasoreactivity testing performed during RHC (to identify rare calcium-channel-blocker responders)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be mildly elevated from secondary erythrocytosis in chronic hypoxemia; not diagnostic
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: Often normal; may be mildly elevated if chronic hypoxemia; not diagnostic
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Normal (no evidence of hemolysis/bleeding as primary driver of symptoms)
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal (argues against bacterial pneumonia/eosinophilic pneumonia if no leukocytosis)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Usually normal (may be normal-to-mildly low in advanced right heart failure/congestion; not diagnostic)
expected_lab_results.Total bilirubin: Normal (may be mildly elevated in advanced right-sided heart failure with hepatic congestion; not diagnostic)

medications: Furosemide 20-40 mg PO daily (titrate to euvolemia if volume overloaded/right heart failure)
Spironolactone 25 mg PO daily (adjunct diuresis if congestion; monitor K+/renal function)
Oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥ 92% (or PaO2 > 60 mmHg) if hypoxemic
Ambrisentan 5 mg PO daily (increase to 10 mg daily as tolerated) OR Macitentan 10 mg PO daily
Tadalafil 40 mg PO daily OR Sildenafil 20 mg PO three times daily
Epoprostenol IV continuous infusion (initiate in high-risk/WHO FC IV or rapidly progressive disease) OR Treprostinil IV/SC/inhaled per specialty
Warfarin (dose per INR goal individualized; consider only in select idiopathic PAH patients per specialist—avoid routine use without clear indication)
Calcium channel blocker (only if acute vasoreactivity positive on RHC): Amlodipine 10-20 mg PO daily OR Diltiazem ER 240-720 mg PO daily (agent depends on baseline heart rate)
non_pharmacological: Refer urgently to a pulmonary hypertension specialty center for risk stratification and initiation/titration of PAH-specific therapy
Baseline and serial functional assessment (WHO functional class, 6-minute walk distance), BNP/NT-proBNP, and echocardiography per specialist
Vaccinations: annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccination
Supervised exercise/rehabilitation as tolerated; avoid heavy isometric exertion
Avoid pregnancy; provide contraception counseling (PAH is high maternal mortality; endothelin receptor antagonists are teratogenic)
Low-sodium diet and daily weights if fluid retention
Screen for secondary causes (connective tissue disease, HIV, portal HTN, drugs/toxins) and for heritable PAH (family history/genetic counseling) as indicated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Specialty follow-up within 1-2 weeks after initiation/escalation of PAH therapy (earlier if high-risk); reassess risk and response every 3 months or sooner if worsening

must_include: PAH is high blood pressure in the lung arteries causing strain on the right side of the heart
Diagnosis requires confirmation with right heart catheterization and exclusion of other causes of pulmonary hypertension
Discuss that symptoms can be nonspecific and that a heart murmur may reflect tricuspid regurgitation from right heart strain
Treatment is risk-based and often uses combination therapy (endothelin receptor antagonist + PDE-5 inhibitor) and may require prostacyclin in severe disease
Diuretics and oxygen treat symptoms/support the heart but do not replace PAH-targeted therapy
If vasoreactivity testing is positive, high-dose calcium channel blockers may be used; otherwise they can be harmful/ineffective
Review red flags needing urgent care (syncope, worsening chest pain, rapidly increasing shortness of breath, signs of right heart failure)
Discuss medication risks and monitoring (blood pressure, edema, liver tests/hemoglobin for endothelin receptor antagonists per agent; site/infection risks for IV prostacyclin)
Reinforce penicillin allergy documentation and that it matters if future antibiotics are needed (e.g., if pneumonia is suspected)
Avoid pregnancy; discuss reliable contraception and teratogenicity risk with endothelin receptor antagonists
Encourage graded activity and avoidance of heavy straining; consider pulmonary rehab
Vaccinations and infection prevention; seek early evaluation for respiratory infections
Low-salt diet, fluid management, daily weights if swelling occurs

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key PAH history: exertional dyspnea, fatigue, chest pain, syncope/presyncope, palpitations, edema/abdominal distension; onset/progression; functional limitation.

Assess secondary causes/risk factors to support idiopathic PAH vs other pulmonary hypertension: connective tissue disease symptoms, congenital heart disease, portal HTN/liver disease, HIV risk, drug/toxin exposure (amphetamines), family history, thromboembolic history, sleep apnea, lung disease symptoms.
Medication review and allergy history (including penicillin allergy details) to prepare for safe prescribing and future infection treatment.

Order baseline evaluation to support diagnosis and treatment planning: BNP/NT-proBNP, CBC, CMP/LFTs, TSH; pregnancy test if of childbearing potential; HIV; autoimmune screen as indicated; iron studies if anemia suspected.

Order diagnostic tests typically required before labeling idiopathic PAH and to exclude other causes: transthoracic echocardiogram, ECG, chest imaging, PFTs, and V/Q scan (or CTPA per protocol) to exclude CTEPH.
Review results for evidence of pulmonary hypertension/right heart strain and to rule out alternative etiologies (lung disease, left heart disease, thromboembolic disease).
Order/arrange right heart catheterization with vasoreactivity testing (when appropriate) to confirm PAH hemodynamics and guide therapy (CCB only if vasoreactive).

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute viral gastroenteritis with dehydration
acceptable_alternatives: Foodborne gastroenteritis (noninflammatory)

required_evidence: Acute onset nausea/vomiting and/or watery diarrhea (typically <7 days)
Clinical signs of dehydration (thirst, dry mucous membranes, orthostasis, reduced urine output)
Laboratory pattern consistent with dehydration (elevated BUN with relatively normal creatinine if available; possible mild electrolyte abnormalities)
No evidence of invasive bacterial process requiring antibiotics (e.g., no high fever, no bloody stools, no severe abdominal pain, no severe leukocytosis)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (e.g., 7-12 x10^3/µL); marked leukocytosis would be atypical for uncomplicated viral gastroenteritis
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Normal to mildly elevated due to hemoconcentration
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Normal to mildly low or mildly high depending on free-water balance (commonly 130-145 mmol/L)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Low-normal to low due to GI losses (e.g., 3.0-3.5 mmol/L possible)
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Often low-normal; may be elevated with non-anion gap metabolic acidosis from diarrhea
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (e.g., 16-22 mmol/L) consistent with metabolic acidosis from diarrhea; may be normal if vomiting-predominant
expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated (e.g., >20 mg/dL) consistent with prerenal azotemia from dehydration

medications: Oral rehydration solution 250-500 mL PO after each loose stool (or small frequent sips as tolerated)
Ondansetron ODT 4 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea/vomiting (max 24 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Assess severity of dehydration; if unable to tolerate PO or signs of severe dehydration, initiate IV isotonic fluids (e.g., normal saline) and urgent evaluation
Diet as tolerated; avoid alcohol and high-fat foods; continue breastfeeding if applicable
Hand hygiene and infection control; avoid food preparation for others until symptoms resolve
Return precautions for red flags: bloody stools, persistent high fever, severe abdominal pain, confusion, syncope, inability to keep fluids down, minimal urine output
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours (sooner same day if worsening, unable to tolerate oral intake, or signs of severe dehydration)

must_include: Likely self-limited stomach/intestinal infection causing fluid losses

Rest and gradual return to normal diet as tolerated

Screen for other sleep disorders and high-risk features: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, morning headaches, excessive sleepiness), restless legs/PLMD, parasomnias; assess suicidality if depression suspected.

Make diagnosis of insomnia based on sleep difficulty plus daytime impairment after considering contributors/rule-outs; determine whether likely primary vs comorbid/secondary.
Explain diagnosis and set expectations that CBT-I is first-line and effective; introduce sleep diary (1–2 weeks) and key sleep hygiene/stimulus control principles.
Explain treatment plan: CBT-I components (stimulus control, sleep restriction/time-in-bed, cognitive strategies) and lifestyle guidance (consistent wake time, caffeine/alcohol timing, screens/light).
Before any prescribing, verify medication allergies/intolerances to proposed agents.
Before prescribing, check interactions with current meds/substances (notably alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, antihistamines, other sedatives).

Collect key contraindication data needed for safe hypnotic selection (pregnancy/breastfeeding, severe COPD/OSA risk, liver disease, history of substance use disorder, older adult fall risk, glaucoma/urinary retention if considering anticholinergics).
If new contraindication-relevant meds/substances are disclosed in the prior step, re-check interactions with the finalized candidate medication.
If patient preference and severity warrant short-term pharmacotherapy, prescribe lowest effective dose for shortest duration with clear stop/reassess plan; otherwise proceed with CBT-I only.

Medication counseling (if prescribed): next-day sedation/dizziness, avoid alcohol/other sedatives, avoid driving/operating machinery if drowsy; agent-specific effects (melatonin vivid dreams; doxepin anticholinergic effects at higher doses).
Arrange follow-up in 2–4 weeks (earlier if safety concerns) to review sleep diary, CBT-I adherence, response, and adverse effects; reassess need for medication.
Close encounter with return precautions (worsening daytime impairment, depressive symptoms/suicidality, signs of sleep apnea, adverse drug effects).

acceptable_alternatives: Cyclic hematopoiesis with recurrent infection

expected_lab_results.WBC: Low during nadir (leukopenia), typically driven by neutropenia
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Markedly decreased during nadir (often <0.5 x10^9/L; may be <0.2 x10^9/L in severe episodes)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal or mildly decreased; may show mild cyclic variation
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: Usually normal or mildly decreased, paralleling hemoglobin
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Often normal; may show mild cyclic fluctuation in cyclic hematopoiesis
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Often normal; may fluctuate with cyclic marrow activity
expected_lab_results.MCV: Typically normal (normocytic) unless another process present
expected_lab_results.C-reactive protein (CRP): Elevated with active respiratory infection (may be normal early or with mild/viral illness)

non_pharmacological: Assess severity and disposition: if fever (≥38.3°C once or ≥38.0°C for ≥1 hour) with ANC <0.5 x10^9/L, treat as febrile neutropenia and send to ED for immediate IV broad-spectrum anti-pseudomonal antibiotics and cultures

Coordinate hematology follow-up for confirmation/monitoring of cyclic pattern and long-term G-CSF strategy
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 24 hours if outpatient; immediate ED evaluation if any fever, worsening respiratory symptoms, hypoxia, or ANC <0.5 x10^9/L; hematology follow-up within 1-2 weeks for cycle tracking and G-CSF titration

must_include: Cyclic neutropenia/cyclic hematopoiesis causes predictable periodic drops in neutrophils that increase infection risk

Rationale for G-CSF (to shorten/lessen neutropenic nadir and reduce infections) and how/when it is administered
When antibiotics are indicated vs when supportive care is sufficient (based on severity and suspicion for bacterial infection)

Review ANC and other results to determine whether patient is in neutropenic nadir and whether criteria for urgent evaluation/admission or empiric antibiotics are met.

Synthesize cyclic hematopoiesis context with current respiratory symptoms and lab results (e.g., viral URI vs bacterial infection, with/without neutropenia; identify febrile neutropenia if fever + ANC low).
Perform required safety check before prescribing; confirm amoxicillin allergy details (urticarial rash) and avoid penicillins/related beta-lactams as appropriate.
Optional safety check before prescribing; screen for interactions (e.g., QT-prolonging agents if considering fluoroquinolone, anticoagulants, cations affecting absorption).

If indicated, prescribe G-CSF per clinical scenario and/or appropriate non-penicillin antibiotic options; avoid antibiotics when supportive care is sufficient; include clear ED precautions for fever during neutropenia.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment rationale, red flags, infection prevention during nadir, adverse effects (G-CSF, fluoroquinolones if used), and shared decision-making on outpatient vs inpatient management.
Arrange timely follow-up and lab monitoring to document cycling, reassess symptoms, and adjust prophylaxis/treatment plan; ensure clear return precautions.
Close encounter after safety-netting and follow-up plan are established.

Transfuse packed RBCs only if symptomatic anemia or hemodynamic compromise (use restrictive strategy and reassess clinically)

Transfusion is not routine and is reserved for significant symptoms or very low hemoglobin
Splenectomy is usually avoided because it can increase clot risk in this disorder
Review warning symptoms requiring urgent care (worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, fainting, dark urine, severe fatigue)
Discuss family history and potential need for family member evaluation/genetic counseling
Clarify penicillin allergy and document reaction (urticarial rash) before any antibiotic prescribing
Maintain good hydration and avoid triggers for hemolysis when applicable (individualized)
Adhere to follow-up labs/appointments to monitor anemia and hemolysis

profile.occupation: account manager at a logistics company

patient.chief_complaint: I can’t stop peeing and I’m thirsty all the time.

volunteer: I can’t stop peeing and I’m thirsty all the time.
I’m getting up 5–7 times a night to use the bathroom.
My mouth feels dry even when I keep drinking water.
This started pretty suddenly over the last couple of weeks.
if_asked: I’m drinking huge amounts of water—my bottle is constantly empty.
My pee looks really clear, like water, and there’s a lot of it each time.
If I can’t drink for even a short while, I feel shaky and get a bad headache.
I’ve felt more tired and foggy lately, especially at work.
I’ve had some nausea a couple of times when I got really thirsty.
I’ve lost a few pounds without trying.
hidden: I had a mild head injury a month ago and felt ‘fine’ afterward, so I didn’t think it mattered.
I’ve been craving ice-cold water and keep it by my bed so I can drink all night.
My lips are cracking and my skin feels drier than usual.
I’ve been getting lightheaded when I stand up quickly.
I’ve been avoiding long meetings or car rides because I’m worried I won’t find a bathroom in time.
resistant: I’ve had a couple of accidents where I didn’t make it to the bathroom in time, and it’s embarrassing.
My sex drive has been lower lately, but I didn’t want to bring that up.
I’ve been feeling more irritable and snappy because I’m exhausted and constantly thirsty.
misleading: I’m worried this is diabetes because my aunt has it.
I’ve been drinking more coffee and energy drinks lately to stay awake, so maybe that’s why I pee so much.
I started a ‘detox’/hydration challenge online and thought maybe my body is just adjusting.
noise: My seasonal allergies have been acting up with sneezing and itchy eyes.
I’ve had some mild lower back soreness after helping a friend move.
I get occasional heartburn after spicy food.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked what the urine looks like and whether the amount seems large.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked what happens if they try to limit fluids or can’t access water.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed if the clinician asks gently about urgency, leakage, or accidents and normalizes the question.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I’m worried this is diabetes because my aunt has it.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked what the patient thinks might be going on or what they’re most worried about.

general_concerns: Believes diabetes insipidus is the same as diabetes mellitus and worries she will "need insulin" or develop complications like blindness if her sugars aren't controlled.
Thinks constant thirst means she should drink as much water as possible at all times, even when she is already urinating frequently, and worries that limiting intake is always dangerous.
Assumes her current medication (meclocycline) is a hormone replacement and expects it to "fix" the underlying pituitary problem permanently.
Believes missing one dose will cause immediate kidney damage or a medical emergency, so she sometimes double-doses if she forgets whether she took it.
Attributes symptoms mainly to stress/caffeine and is unsure why a brain/pituitary issue would affect urination.
economic_concerns: Worries that repeated lab tests (electrolytes, osmolality) and imaging follow-ups will become unaffordable over time and asks if monitoring can be reduced.
Concerned about taking time off work for frequent appointments because her job is performance-based and she fears lost income/bonuses.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking account manager. Open with: “I can’t stop peeing and I’m thirsty all the time,” plus: sudden onset over 2 weeks, up 5–7 times nightly, dry mouth despite drinking. If asked, add: drinking huge amounts; urine very clear and large volume; if you can’t drink you feel shaky with a bad headache; fatigue/brain fog at work; occasional nausea when very thirsty; a few pounds unintentional weight loss. Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about injuries/neurologic history: mild head injury 1 month ago. Keep hidden unless prompted: ice-cold water by bed, cracked lips/dry skin, lightheaded on standing, avoiding meetings/car rides due to bathroom fear. Resist/only reveal if clinician builds rapport: a couple accidents, lower sex drive, irritability/snappiness. Express misconceptions: worry about diabetes (aunt has it); wonder if coffee/energy drinks or an online “detox/hydration challenge” explains it. If shown empathy, soften, admit embarrassment and anxiety, and ask about keeping tests/visits affordable and minimizing time off work.

medications: Meclocycline 300 mg PO twice daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for headache
comorbidities: History of mild traumatic brain injury (6 months ago) with persistent headaches
confounders: Meclocycline can cause nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and worsen polyuria, complicating differentiation from central DI
High caffeine intake (4-5 cups coffee/day) contributing to diuresis
Recent use of OTC diuretic 'water pills' (intermittent) reported by patient
Anxiety-related polydipsia raising consideration of primary polydipsia

correct_diagnosis.primary: Central diabetes insipidus
acceptable_alternatives: Diabetes insipidus (unspecified type) pending confirmatory testing
Partial central diabetes insipidus
required_evidence: Polyuria with 24-hour urine volume typically >3 L/day (often much higher) with dilute urine (low urine specific gravity and low urine osmolality)
Serum osmolality high-normal or elevated with serum sodium high-normal or elevated in the setting of free-water loss
Normal fasting serum glucose to exclude osmotic diuresis from diabetes mellitus
Renal function not severely impaired (BUN/creatinine not explaining polyuria)
Clinical response to desmopressin (rise in urine osmolality and fall in urine volume) consistent with central DI (if performed)

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium (Na+): High-normal to elevated (often >145 mmol/L if water intake does not match losses)
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: Elevated (typically >295 mOsm/kg) or high-normal with ongoing free-water loss
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality (spot): Low/inappropriately dilute for serum osmolality (often <300 mOsm/kg; frequently <200 in complete DI)
expected_lab_results.Serum glucose (fasting): Normal (to rule out osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia)
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be mildly increased from dehydration or normal
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Usually normal unless concurrent kidney disease; may rise slightly with dehydration

medications: Desmopressin 0.05 mg PO twice daily (titrate to lowest effective dose) OR desmopressin 10 mcg intranasal once daily to twice daily
If inpatient and unable to take PO: desmopressin 1-2 mcg IV/SC every 12-24 hours (titrate carefully)
non_pharmacological: Ensure free access to water and match intake to thirst; avoid fluid restriction unless supervised for hyponatremia risk
If hypernatremic/dehydrated: replace free water (PO/enteral preferred; IV D5W if needed) with cautious correction of sodium
Monitor strict input/output and daily weights; trend serum sodium and osmolality during initiation/titration
Identify and address underlying cause of central DI (e.g., pituitary/hypothalamic pathology) with appropriate specialist referral/imaging as indicated
Avoid using meclocycline for central DI (it is not indicated and can cause nephrotoxicity); discontinue if being used for this purpose
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck serum sodium and symptoms within 24-72 hours after starting/changing desmopressin; outpatient follow-up in 1-2 weeks for dose titration and ongoing monitoring

must_include: DI is due to insufficient antidiuretic hormone (ADH/vasopressin) effect causing inability to concentrate urine
This leads to excessive dilute urination and risk of dehydration and high sodium
Different from diabetes mellitus; blood sugar is not the cause
Desmopressin replaces ADH and reduces urine volume and thirst
Dose is individualized; goal is symptom control while avoiding low sodium
Key warning: over-treatment can cause water retention and hyponatremia—symptoms include headache, nausea, confusion, seizures
When to seek urgent care: confusion, severe headache, seizures, inability to keep fluids down, signs of severe dehydration
Plan for monitoring sodium and urine output during dose changes
Discuss current meclocycline use: not appropriate for central DI and potential kidney toxicity; rationale for stopping/changing therapy
Maintain access to water and drink to thirst; avoid excessive forced drinking once on desmopressin
Keep a log of urine volume, thirst, and daily weight during titration
Review sick-day guidance: if reduced intake/vomiting/diarrhea, hold or reduce desmopressin and contact clinician to avoid hyponatremia

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key symptoms, duration, fluid intake, urine volume/nocturia, thirst, triggers (head injury, neurosurgery), pregnancy status, and current meds (including meclocycline/lithium/diuretics) to frame suspected central DI and rule out emergencies.

Order initial evaluation labs to support DI diagnosis and assess safety prior to therapy: serum sodium, serum osmolality, glucose, BUN/Cr, urine osmolality and specific gravity (and consider urine volume/24h).
Confirm pattern consistent with DI (e.g., high/normal-high serum osmolality, inappropriately dilute urine) and assess current sodium/renal function to guide urgency and desmopressin initiation.
Make or affirm diagnosis of central diabetes insipidus (and explicitly distinguish from diabetes mellitus).
Critical safety check before prescribing desmopressin or any alternative medication.
Critical safety check for drug interactions that increase hyponatremia risk (e.g., SSRIs, carbamazepine, thiazides, NSAIDs) or affect renal handling of water/sodium.
Critical safety check for current meclocycline use (nephrotoxicity risk; inappropriate for central DI) and any other interacting agents.
Critical safety check for desmopressin contraindications/precautions (hyponatremia, significant renal impairment, polydipsia, uncontrolled heart failure).

Initiate desmopressin with individualized dosing plan and explicit sodium-safety strategy (avoid over-treatment). Discontinue/plan to stop meclocycline if present unless a compelling alternative indication is clarified.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment and hyponatremia warning signs, address concerns/urgent symptoms, monitoring plan, lifestyle advice including sick-day guidance and logging weight/urine/thirst.
Arrange timely follow-up and lab monitoring (serum sodium and clinical response) especially during titration or medication changes.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-net instructions, and follow-up plan.

minimal_paths.description: Stable outpatient with polyuria/polydipsia; labs support central DI; initiate desmopressin with monitoring and education; stop meclocycline.
must_collect: current medications including meclocycline and hyponatremia-risk drugs
fluid intake pattern (drink to thirst vs forced intake)
red flags (confusion, severe headache, seizures, vomiting/diarrhea, inability to hydrate)
steps: ASK: characterize polyuria/polydipsia, nocturia, triggers, meds (meclocycline), comorbidities, red flags
ORDER_LAB: serum sodium, serum osmolality, glucose, BUN/Cr, urine osmolality and specific gravity (± 24h urine volume)
GET_RESULTS: verify dilute urine with inappropriately low urine osmolality/SG and assess sodium/renal function
DIAGNOSE: central diabetes insipidus; distinguish from diabetes mellitus
CHECK_INTERACTION: review meds increasing hyponatremia risk and meclocycline
CHECK_CONTRAINDICATION: baseline hyponatremia, significant renal impairment, polydipsia/heart failure considerations
PRESCRIBE: desmopressin with conservative start and titration plan; stop/plan to discontinue meclocycline for central DI
EDUCATE: diagnosis, treatment, hyponatremia warning signs, urgent care triggers, drink-to-thirst, logging, sick-day rules, sodium monitoring plan
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: sodium check and symptom review shortly after initiation/dose change; ongoing follow-up

minimal_paths.description: Patient has signs of significant dehydration or hypernatremia risk; prioritize urgent evaluation while still completing critical prescribing safety if desmopressin is started.
must_collect: orthostatic symptoms, severe thirst, reduced oral intake
vomiting/diarrhea or inability to keep fluids down
current meds (meclocycline) and comorbid renal/cardiac disease
steps: ASK: screen for severe dehydration/hypernatremia symptoms and inability to hydrate; identify meclocycline and other meds
ORDER_LAB: urgent serum sodium, serum osmolality, BUN/Cr, glucose; urine osmolality/SG if feasible
GET_RESULTS: confirm severity (e.g., elevated sodium/azotemia) and determine need for ED/urgent care
DIAGNOSE: central DI suspected/confirmed based on pattern
EDUCATE: urgent care referral criteria and immediate safety steps (do not force water if vomiting; seek ED)
PRESCRIBE: only if appropriate in setting and with monitoring plan; discontinue/avoid meclocycline for central DI
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: expedited follow-up and sodium monitoring

acceptable_variants.description: Agent asks for and orders a 24-hour urine volume and/or copeptin/water-deprivation test referral rather than making a definitive diagnosis immediately, but still provides provisional counseling and avoids unsafe prescribing without safety checks.

Agent performs safety checks immediately before PRESCRIBE (after DIAGNOSE) and combines interaction review into a single interaction check action; acceptable if all three required checks occur before prescribing.

Agent educates earlier (after DIAGNOSE) and then repeats key warnings after prescribing; acceptable if all required communication milestones are covered by the end and safety checks precede prescribing.
